title: Peruvian Fish Ceviche

ingredients.name: firm fresh fish (seer fish, tilapia or pomfret), skinless, cubed

subs.name: prawns or squid cut into rings work beautifully — prawns need 15–20 minutes in lime to cure fully
a mix of lemon and raw mango juice for a strongly Indian flavour profile

ingredients.name: green chilli, finely sliced (ají limo substitute)

subs.name: a small amount of red chilli powder mixed with lime mimics the ají limo heat without the fresh chilli crunch

ingredients.name: ginger, minced
visual_checks: not traditional but popular in Indian coastal versions
ingredients.notes: not traditional but popular in Indian coastal versions

ingredients.name: corn on the cob, boiled and cut into rounds

steps.text: Ensure the fish is very fresh — this dish is only as good as the fish you use. Cut it into 2–3 cm cubes, removing any remaining bones. Rinse under cold water and pat dry.

Thinly slice the red onion and rinse under cold running water for 2 minutes to remove harshness. Drain and squeeze out excess moisture. This step is important — raw onion without rinsing overpowers the dish.

Place the fish cubes in a non-reactive bowl (glass or ceramic, not metal). Season generously with salt and toss well — the salt starts the texture-firming process. Add the lime juice, minced garlic and ginger (if using) and stir gently to combine.

Cover and refrigerate for 10–15 minutes. The fish will turn opaque and firm as the citric acid denatures the proteins — this is 'cooking' with acid. Check at 10 minutes: the outside should be white and opaque but the very centre should still have a slightly translucent core for the best texture.

Add the rinsed onion slices, sliced green chilli and torn coriander leaves. Toss gently and taste — adjust salt, lime and chilli. Serve immediately in chilled bowls with boiled corn rounds alongside. The leche de tigre (the leftover lime-fish juice in the bowl) is considered a delicacy and can be sipped as a shot.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → prawns or squid cut into rings work beautifully — prawns need 15–20 minutes in lime to cure fully; lemon → a mix of lemon and raw mango juice for a strongly Indian flavour profile; green-chilli → a small amount of red chilli powder mixed with lime mimics the ají limo heat without the fresh chilli crunch
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Fresh seer fish (vanjaram), tilapia or pomfret from the fish market are the ideal choices for Indian ceviche — all firm enough to hold their shape during curing. Use the abundant Indian nimbu (lime) for authenticity; it works perfectly. The concept of acid-curing fish is unfamiliar to most Indian cooks but requires zero equipment. Serve as an appetiser at parties or as a light summer lunch. Always use fish purchased the same day.
